name: Golden Pothos
description: An easy-care trailing vine with golden-variegated heart-shaped leaves. Perfect for beginners.
details: Golden Pothos is one of the most popular houseplants for a reason — it thrives on neglect and looks stunning doing it. The heart-shaped leaves trail beautifully from shelves and hanging baskets, growing several feet long over time. It purifies indoor air and adapts to almost any lighting condition, making it the ultimate starter plant.
care: Low to bright indirect light. Water when top inch of soil is dry. Tolerates low humidity but appreciates occasional misting. Fertilize monthly during spring and summer.

name: Snake Plant
description: A hardy, upright plant with striking sword-shaped leaves. Virtually indestructible.
details: Snake Plants are the ultimate low-maintenance houseplant. Their tall, architectural leaves add a modern touch to any room and they're one of the best air-purifying plants out there. They store water in their thick leaves so they can go weeks without watering — perfect if you travel or tend to forget.
care: Low to bright indirect light. Water every 2-3 weeks, letting soil dry completely between waterings. Avoid overwatering — they'd rather be dry than soggy. No humidity requirements.

name: English Lavender
description: Fragrant purple blooms that attract pollinators. Great for borders and containers.
details: English Lavender brings that classic cottage garden charm with its silvery foliage and fragrant purple flower spikes. It's a pollinator magnet — bees and butterflies love it. Harvest the blooms for sachets, cooking, or just enjoy the scent every time you walk by. It's drought-tolerant once established and does great in Kentucky's climate.
care: Full sun (6+ hours). Well-drained soil — don't let it sit in water. Drought tolerant once established. Prune after flowering to keep it bushy. Hardy through Kentucky winters.

name: Echeveria
description: A rosette-forming succulent with beautiful pastel-colored fleshy leaves.
details: Echeveria are the picture-perfect succulents — tight rosettes of thick, fleshy leaves in shades of green, pink, purple, and blue. They're compact enough for windowsills and desks, and they produce offsets (baby plants) that you can separate and share with friends. Great for arrangements and terrariums.
care: Bright direct light — at least 4-6 hours of sun. Water sparingly every 1-2 weeks, soaking the soil and letting it dry completely. Never let water sit in the rosette. Use well-draining succulent soil.

name: Monstera Deliciosa
description: The iconic Swiss cheese plant with large, fenestrated leaves. A real showstopper.
details: The Monstera Deliciosa is the statement plant that takes any room from ordinary to tropical. Those iconic split leaves (called fenestrations) develop as the plant matures — younger leaves start solid and develop holes over time. It's a climbing plant in the wild, so give it a moss pole or trellis and watch it take off. A true conversation starter.
care: Bright indirect light — avoid direct sun which can burn leaves. Water when top 2 inches of soil are dry. Loves humidity — mist regularly or use a humidifier. Feed monthly in growing season. Wipe leaves to keep them shiny.

name: Cherry Tomato Starter
description: Ready-to-plant cherry tomato seedlings, perfect for Kentucky gardens.
details: Our cherry tomato starters are hardened off and ready to go straight into your garden or a large container on your patio. Cherry tomatoes are the most rewarding vegetable to grow — they produce handfuls of sweet, bite-sized fruit all summer long. Kids love picking them right off the vine. Varieties may vary by season.
care: Full sun (8+ hours is ideal). Water deeply and regularly — consistent moisture prevents cracking. Stake or cage for support as they grow. Fertilize every 2 weeks once fruiting begins. Plant after last frost (mid-April in Kentucky).

name: Boston Fern
description: A classic hanging plant with graceful, arching fronds. Brings life to any porch.
details: Boston Ferns are the quintessential porch plant — those lush, arching fronds draping from a hanging basket are a Southern classic for a reason. They soften any space and add instant charm. They do best in humid environments, so they're perfect for covered porches, bathrooms, or kitchens. They're also great air purifiers.
care: Indirect light — no direct sun. Keep soil consistently moist but not waterlogged. Loves humidity — mist daily or set on a pebble tray. They drop leaves when stressed, but bounce back quickly. Bring indoors before first frost.

name: Blue Hydrangea
description: Stunning blue mophead blooms that light up any garden. A Kentucky favorite.
details: Blue Hydrangeas are one of the most beloved garden shrubs in Kentucky — those big, showy mophead blooms make a statement from late spring through fall. Here's the fun part: the soil pH determines the color. Acidic soil gives you blue, alkaline soil gives you pink. Add aluminum sulfate to keep them blue. They make gorgeous cut flower arrangements too.
care: Partial shade — morning sun with afternoon shade is ideal. Keep soil consistently moist, especially in summer heat. Acidic soil (pH 5.2-5.5) keeps blooms blue. Mulch to retain moisture. Prune in late winter — they bloom on old wood. Hardy in Kentucky.
